did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a multi-task deep learning framework based on an EfficientNet backbone and Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) to perform lesion segmentation, dermoscopic feature detection, and classification of skin cancer (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis) using the ISIC 2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a unified multi-task deep learning framework (EfficientNet-FPN) for skin cancer segmentation and classification. It details specific methodology, dataset usage (ISIC 2017), training procedures, and reports new quantitative results (e.g., 75.8% accuracy, Dice scores > 0.83) on a test set, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Segmentation and Classification Using Multi-Task Deep Learning with EfficientNet and Feature Pyramid Network
journal: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
brief_summary: This study proposes a unified multi-task deep learning framework to simultaneously perform lesion segmentation, dermoscopic feature detection, and classification of melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis. The architecture utilizes an EfficientNet backbone with a Feature Pyramid Network and a U-Net-style decoder, trained on the imbalanced ISIC 2017 dataset using dynamic oversampling. Evaluation on the official test set demonstrated a classification accuracy of 75.8% with a macro F1-score of 0.6990. The segmentation branch achieved Dice scores above 0.83, while feature detection yielded a mean F1-score of approximately 0.60. These results indicate that sharing feature representations across tasks improves generalization and stability compared to siloed approaches.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant class in the ISIC 2017 dataset; minority class (19% of dataset) requiring dynamic oversampling; classification F1-score of 0.5911 on test set.
Benign class in the ISIC 2017 dataset; majority class (68% of dataset); classification F1-score of 0.8391 on test set; highest confusion with melanoma.
Benign class in the ISIC 2017 dataset; minority class (13% of dataset); classification F1-score of 0.6667 on test set.

all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin lesions into three categories: melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis. Evaluated using accuracy (75.8% on test set) and macro F1-score (0.6990 on test set).
Binary segmentation of lesion regions from dermoscopic images using a U-Net-style decoder. Evaluated using Dice coefficient (above 0.83) and Intersection over Union (IoU).
Multi-label detection of seven key dermoscopic structures (e.g., pigment network, streaks, globules) from JSON annotations. Evaluated using macro F1-score (mean score of 0.59 on test set).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset for skin lesion analysis containing dermoscopic images with associated binary segmentation masks, JSON files for dermoscopic feature detection (presence of seven key structures), and disease classification labels. The dataset is known for class imbalance, particularly with melanoma being a minority class.

use.training: Used for training the multi-task deep learning framework (EfficientNet-FPN) with dynamic oversampling and on-the-fly augmentation to handle class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation during training to monitor metrics such as accuracy (reaching 84%) and Dice coefficient (stabilizing above 0.83).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance, reporting classification accuracy (75.8%), macro F1-score (0.6990), segmentation Dice scores (>0.83), and feature detection F1-scores (~0.60).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, Dice coefficient, and AUC.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-task deep learning framework based on EfficientNet-B4 backbone with Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)
architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNet-B4 was used as a pre-trained model for the shared encoder

architecture_details.feature_aggregation: Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) blending information across scales
architecture_details.segmentation_head: U-Net-style decoder with successive bilinear upsampling layers and convolutional blocks, ending with 1x1 convolution and Sigmoid activation
architecture_details.classification_head: Global average pooling on deepest backbone feature map, followed by fully connected layer with ReLU activation, Dropout regularization, and linear classifier for 3 classes
architecture_details.feature_detection_head: Global average pooling on shared features, followed by fully connected layer outputting 7-dimensional vector with Sigmoid activations for multi-label detection

loss_functions.segmentation: Combined Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) and Dice loss
loss_functions.total_loss: Weighted sum: L_total = 0.5 * (L_BCE_seg + L_Dice) + 0.5 * (L_CE_cls + L_BCE_feat) with weights w_seg = w_cls = w_feat = 1

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named, but 'standard deep-learning training loop' mentioned
training_details.batch_sampling: WeightedRandomSampler with inverse-frequency weights to handle class imbalance
training_details.augmentation: On-the-fly augmentation via Albumentations (horizontal/vertical flips, random 90 rotations, shift-scale-rotate perturbations, brightness/contrast adjustments, hue/saturation modifications, Gaussian blur/noise, coarse dropout)
training_details.reproducibility: Fixed random seeds for NumPy, PyTorch, and CUDA; deterministic modes enabled

tasks: Disease classification (3 classes: melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Hybrid model (referenced in comparison with Fiaz [12])
ML (Machine Learning, referenced in comparison with Nasir [5])
ViT (Vision Transformer, referenced in comparison with Dagnaw [6])
Transformer-style models (referenced as potential future work)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing segmentation masks to match input dimensions to maintain spatial correspondence
Normalization of images to fit EfficientNet input format using ImageNet statistics

On-the-fly data augmentation via Albumentations library including: horizontal and vertical flips, random 90-degree rotations, shift-scale-rotate perturbations, brightness/contrast adjustments, hue/saturation modifications, Gaussian blur and noise, and coarse dropout
Dynamic oversampling strategy with continuous augmentation to handle class imbalance
WeightedRandomSampler with inverse-frequency weights for batch sampling

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal and vertical flips; random 90-degree rotations; shift-scale-rotate perturbations.
Brightness and contrast adjustments; hue and saturation modifications.
On-the-fly augmentation performed during training using the Albumentations library to increase visual diversity for minority classes without modifying the original dataset.
Dynamic oversampling strategy combined with continuous on-the-fly augmentation to provide more variation for minority classes (specifically melanoma) during training.

conclusions: The proposed multi-task deep learning framework, utilizing an EfficientNet backbone with a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) and a U-Net-style decoder, successfully performs lesion segmentation, dermoscopic feature detection, and classification simultaneously.
Sharing feature representations across segmentation, feature detection, and classification tasks leads to better generalization and stability compared to siloed or separate pipeline approaches.

The framework achieved a classification accuracy of 75.8% and a macro F1-score of 0.6990 on the ISIC 2017 test set, with segmentation Dice scores stabilizing above 0.83 and feature detection yielding a mean F1-score of approximately 0.60.

Dynamic oversampling combined with on-the-fly augmentation effectively addressed the severe class imbalance in the ISIC 2017 dataset, particularly improving stability and sensitivity for the minority melanoma class.
The highest classification confusion occurred between melanoma and nevus, reflecting the visual similarity of these lesion types in dermoscopic imaging.

The unified framework reduces computational redundancy and maintains efficiency while providing interpretability advantages through joint feature detection, which is often absent in single-task classification pipelines.
The study establishes a foundation for future improvements in clinical decision support, including potential integration with transformer-based models, semi-supervised training, and explainable AI.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2017 dataset, which exhibits severe class imbalance (Nevus ~68%, Melanoma ~19%, Seborrheic Keratosis ~13%), potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or datasets.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated only on the official ISIC 2017 test set, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific benchmark.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the performance improvements over baseline or siloed approaches.

Classification performance for the minority class (Melanoma) was notably lower (F1-score 0.5911) compared to benign classes (Nevus F1 0.8391), indicating persistent difficulty in distinguishing melanoma from nevus despite oversampling.
The highest classification confusion occurred between melanoma and nevus, reflecting inherent visual similarities in dermoscopic imaging that the model could not fully resolve.
Feature detection performance was relatively weak (mean F1-score ~0.60), suggesting that the multi-task framework may not sufficiently optimize for fine-grained dermoscopic structure identification.
The paper is a conference publication (ICBSII 2026) with limited scope compared to journal articles, and lacks detailed ablation studies isolating the contribution of each task head.

Deployment concerns include the lack of discussion on computational efficiency metrics (e.g., inference time, FLOPs) despite claims of reduced redundancy, and no mention of clinical integration or regulatory compliance.
